title: 2025/12/02 - EDSI (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-02 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - 2024/2025 Annual review of Financial Markets Authority [Part 2]
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: 2024/2025 Annual review of Financial Markets Authority
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Economic Development Science and Innovation Committee 2024/2025 Annual review of Financial Markets Authority Hearing of evidence: Financial Markets Authority Samantha Barrass, Chief Executive Liam Mason, Executive Director - Evaluation and Oversight and General Counsel Craig Stobo, FMA Board Chair
descriptionSummary: 2024/2025 Annual review of Financial Markets Authority. Financial Markets Authority. Samantha Barrass, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Uh,

so everybody's here. Yeah, we all are here, so we can get started when you're ready. We have one hour with you. So if you would like to start by some, a brief opening remarks. Yes. And then there will be Christians from members. Thank you. Perfect. Thank you. Praise. Thank you. Uh, Dr. Palmier. Uh, good afternoon.

I'm Craig Stobo, chair of the FMA. Uh, thank you very much. Opportunity to speak to you and answer any queries you may have. Uh, it's been a year of progressive, incredibly proud of our work on locking innovation through the regulatory sandbox and helping businesses to operate in new ways.

At the same time, we've worked with the NZX and MB to make stock exchange listings easier, while addressing the global shifts towards private markets with their challenges of valuation and liquidity. These efforts are all about developing and building confidence in our financial markets, ensuring they remain attractive for investors and businesses alike.

It's a collective achievement that reflects the strength of our partnerships and the commitment of everyone involved. With that in mind, I would like to hand over to our chief executive, Samantha Bures. Samantha. Hi, Tina. Coto. Kato. It's a pleasure to be here. I'm joined by Leah Mason, who's our General Counsel and Director of Evaluation and oversight.

Uh, just a few brief remarks. The year, um, has seen us to deliver on our mandate to promote and facilitate fair, efficient, and transparent financial markets. In March, we successfully implemented the conduct of financial institutions regime, which gives us formal oversight over banks, insurers, and non-bank deposit takers.

And this is for the first time, uh, in, in New Zealand that they have been regulated, that they're now being regulated for their conduct. Uh, we launched our first financial conduct report in June, uh, to ensure the industry understood our priorities, uh, with focusing on making us predictable as a regulator, we took enforcement action where consumer harm was evident, pursuing a wide range of cases.

We've taken lead on issues around mortgage fraud and being part of a broader coalition on fighting scams. KiwiSaver turned 18 this year and remains a cornerstone of Kiwi's. Uh, long-term wealth funds under management have now reached 123 billion. We very much welcome policy discussion on the future of Kiwi Saver.

Uh, we note, uh, and are concerned that just under a third of members of working age are not contributing at the moment. Uh, we've begun policy work on custody, and as Kiwi Saver funds, uh, rise substantially, it's important to consider the role of the businesses who actually hold this money, and they're not directly regulated for that purpose.

Internally, we continue to focus on the culture and on our people. This year in review was the first time total FMA staff numbers fell since the FMA was founded in 2011. And I acknowledge the work of our staff during this difficult period. I'm very proud to report that our gender pay gap, uh, which also includes my pay now, sits at half a percent down from almost 10%, uh, two years ago.

So, our ease of doing business survey is a very useful tool for understanding stakeholders views many responses were positive, but also offer some valuable insight on how we can do better looking ahead. There's significant policy work on the horizon. The need for a clear regulatory framework for digital assets and ongoing work to ensure the future of New Zealand's capital markets requires ongoing support and attention.

So, that's it. Thank you very much for your time and very, very much welcome your questions. Thank you. We'll start with Ena Williams. Dear reporter. And hello to you, Mr. Chair. All the way in, somewhere sunny. Um, I, um, look blown away by, um, just how busy the FMA has been this year.

Um, you've canceled licenses, filed criminal charges, taken some action against major institutions. Um, should we be worried that, uh, firms are treating misconduct as low risk in New Zealand? Yeah. Look, are they treating misconduct as low risk?

It is very much the focus of our enforcement activity is to show that whilst we are an engagement led regulator, there are consequences and significant consequences for misconduct. We focus our enforcement action on the most egregious misconduct cases. We have achieved some significant wins through the courts, uh, this, this year.

We have seen our enforcement work, uh, in increase over the past year, and that has been a function of the economic times that we are in, as well as the expansion of our function. Liam, I dunno if you wanted to add anything from Yeah. Well, two, two things. I think, um, the, I think the vast majority of firms, uh, will try to do the right thing.

Um, but incentives is what it's all about. And that's, that's where our supervisory engagement with the firms is, is that is where we change behavior most of the time. Um, what we, what we are seeing as Samantha said, is an unfortunate, uh, number of cases, which are simple fraud and scams.

Um, that's by no means anything other than the, you know, a very small minority of the industry, but it's a minority that has a big impact. And so it is important that we keep up the prevention work, keep up the education work, keep up the enforcement work. Yeah. And we, and we're still seeing in some of our fair dealing work, we're still seeing ongoing lack of investment in systems to secure, you know, what is being offered, um, in, in terms of offerings to customers is, is unable to be delivered because of the systems.

And that that's a, you know, that that's an ongoing issue. That will become an increasing focus for us. We've got more opportunity now. We are directly regulating our banks and insurance companies and non-bank deposit takers for conduct. We have a broader range of tools that we can use. We have thus far, and will until March this year, only been able to deal with issues emanating from those sectors as a matter of enforcement, as a matter of breach of fair dealing requirements, we now have the broader proactive supervisory toolkit

to be able to tackle issues with, with that group of people. I could add something else. Uh, Irina, um, thank you for your question. We are changing our, uh, way we deliver our regulation, which is moving to outcomes focus where we are getting in the boardrooms of businesses addressing governors, stewards of companies, not just at a, uh, legal or compliance level to, uh, ask questions of board members around how they think about risks and harms, and how those harms can be suppressed before they become a problem for all of us.

So it's trying to get ahead of the curve by, by engaging widely with the top echelons of businesses in their board meetings and their senior leadership team meetings, to, to make them aware of what we are interested in, which is why the financial conduct report is so important. Um, thank you. That's helpful.

Just thinking through then those cost implications for consumers, um, you know, thinking about misleading conduct and banking, insurance and financial advice, which, um, from the number of, um, enforcement actions that you've taken in the last year, it seems to me that these are still adding on, um, a huge number of costs to consumers.

Hope Group comes to mind, where consumers were mislead to paying double premiums. Um, how are you dealing with those, uh, number of situations that might be sort of between that proactive supervisory role that you have and enforcement, but are still, um, in the middle here where, um, things are going wrong for consumers and they're paying more for services that should be cheaper? Yeah.

Yeah. Thank, thank you for that. Um, so something, as, as Smith said, a lot of the cases we see are due to underinvestment, uh, in systems. Many of them are long running. That's why, uh, in in our financial conduct report for this past year, we, um, identified a priority for banks and insurers of undertaking product reviews that is going through the back book to see what has been mis-sold, what hasn't been delivered.

Um, we get a lot of self reports and we encourage firms to self-report. Something that, uh, you, you don't see is that the vast majority of these are resolved without enforcement action, but with remediation to customers. Mm-hmm. And, and that's what we need. And the enforcement cases that you've seen go through where there are penalties applied, there's also been remediation to the customers.

And we, we want to get to the point where we're not fixing it up after the event, we're stopping it, but where we are at the moment, we're still going through that remedial stage. And in that middle, that middle group that you identified. And it's a, it's a really good point because the, the formal enforcement cases that we see go through the courts are just a fraction of the issues that we will face.

So there is a middle group where we use our supervisory tools. So we have direction orders, we can have license conditions we do onsite, uh, we will do onsite visits where they will be, uh, requirements to change the way firms, firms are doing things as well. So we've got a broader range of tools, uh, that can be led, that can be used in harder ways.

Uh, that can be far. One of the, one of the challenges with enforcement action taken through the courts is the time lapse between the, uh, problem occurring and when the credible deteriorative starts. And sometimes because of the, you know, it can be 1, 2, 3 years, uh, in, in, in time.

So one of the points that we are working towards as we look upon the outcomes is how we can use the broader range of tools that we have to be able to make our regulation felt at the sharp end, but, but do it faster. Um, I guess that's, um, that's something I'm really interested in, into, and when you've come out with these nine priorities, I think have been quite well socialized with your stakeholders, um, how can we then use that at a parliamentary level to, to expect your progress and your tracking against that, those priorities?

I mean, there are nine of them. There are a lot of them Actually. And, and, and there are more than that, right? There's, there's about 16, 16 regulatory priorities. So this is the first year we've done the financial conduct report. Uh, what, as I, as I said before, what we are aiming to do with that report is to set out the regulatory priorities of things that we're going to be focusing on and key measures of success that we are looking for in that work.

So that there is something that we can, when we do the second financial conduct report in June of 2026, we will report back on how good a job we have done on delivering against those regulatory priorities. We'll be reporting back on those that are multi-year where, but, but we will be specific on the success statements.

So that's, you know, so that it's not kind of, uh, a, a loose, a loose priority. I suspect that we will reduce, I mean, I can't speak the border. We, we know we, we are beginning to do work on our red priority. 16 is a lot. It's, it's been a little bit trial to see how it works.

As our first year, uh, we, we may find that we need to be a little bit more focused in terms of our reg priorities, but certainly at the select committee, select committee level, and at the parliamentary level, I, my, you know, our ambition is that the financial conduct report gives you some very clear outcomes that you can measure us against us to account against in addition to our annual report.

You can, and, and they're, and they're meant to be real and meaningful for your constituents, because ultimately what we are focused on is improving New Zealander's experience in the financial services sector. And the more specific and purposeful we can be, the more we can give you the tools to be able to hold us to account on their behalf for that work.

Mm-hmm. Uh, Uh, first of all, I just want to echo arena's, uh, sentiment, um, heartened, uh, by the developments that you've made over, over just even the last year been fabulous. Um, the C-C-C-F-A coming to you, the Twin Pot Twin Peaks model, within that, a single service provider, having some kind of regulatory control over what the banks do and insurers do, I think is fabulous as part of this kind of new world we are entering into.

So very heartened by all of that. Um, also alongside that, the, the Kofi having that insight and then the group you've put together with the R-B-N-Z-F-M-A, ComCom mb the really integrated and connected approach mm-hmm. Which I think is fabulous. Um, I look forward to how some of those outcomes come about.

One issue that's come up quite recently, which is really significant, is the outcome of a lot of these scams and these frauds are actually money laundering and financing of terrorism. And so our party has been pushing very hard to look at, um, organized crime overseas and how they operate and how we can address that here.

Mm-hmm. At the moment, um, the responses, the prudential responsibility, the regulatory responsibility for A-M-L-C-F-T is worth Reserve Bank and yourselves, and this is moving apparently to DIA. Mm-hmm. Intuitively, I think that's not the right move.

Um, just wondering if you would like to comment on that. So the policy decisions of the government and that decision is, you know, our, our job primarily is to make it work really well. And that is what we are focused on working with the agencies to secure a seamless transition to the, to the DIA.

When that happens, uh, we, even with that happening, we will continue to take, uh, a good citizen systems view. Uh, there is, there, there will be a relationship that we will need to build and secure with the DIA to make sure that the intelligence and the insights that we bring from our scam work and from our oversight of financial services is fed really well into the work that the DIA do.

So even though it will be shifting to the DIA, we are not a siloed organization, so we will, we will be working very hard to make it work incredibly well. Mm-hmm. Because the, some of the reports that come from the banks in terms of suspicious activity, um, at the moment, um, I fear fall into a, with the FIU, et cetera, and we sort of, we know from the inside that probably 80% of them don't even get looked at.

Yeah. So that's, in terms of your comment before around timeliness and addressing the, like scams and fraud and so on, that's a bit of a barrier or a bit of a, um, yeah. Like the whole system to me seems like, yeah. So David, it's best that if we have a question. Yeah.

Uh, so I think, I think the CEO's got it. Okay. Yeah. Thank you. Alright. Good. Um, you have a prime, Scott will, Um, a note that you've, um, you've, well, there's been changes to the climate related disclosures scheme, and I'm wondering what, what you've learned from other jurisdictions, how well that's worked and how well that is working.

Yes, here. Sure. Well, New Zealand was in the unusual position of being a leader in mandatory, uh, climate related disclosures. Mm-hmm. Um, and we, we've completed a first year of monitoring. A lot of effort went into it. Uh, it was a big setup cost for firms. Mm-hmm.

Uh, and we, we were very pleased that the effort that did go in, um, we are seeing overseas jurisdictions catching up, uh, the, the international, um, standards that, uh, ICF has, um, got, its, its standards settled. Australia is following fast.

Um, what we are seeing overseas is that, uh, by and large the thresholds for reporting higher than they were set in New Zealand. And, um, I think that's probably reflected in the, in the recent changes that, uh, the, the proportionate cost of that we, we did see was very high. Uh, also leading out with managed funds, uh, is not something that that other jurisdictions have done.

So we are probably moving closer into alignment, I would say, with where the rest of the, um, I suppose not the rest of the world, that's too grand a statement, but the comparative jurisdictions are, are heading. Um, one of the things that we have seen from some research is that as you move, well, two things.

We understand a number of companies that are currently reporting, we want to continue reporting in some form. What has been observed in some research is that, uh, voluntary reporting can lead to more selective reporting. So we are expecting that we may have to, um, increase our monitoring Yeah.

Of the voluntary reporting sector to make sure that they're giving a, a fair and balanced view of their efforts supplementary. So, So in a sense, are you saying that we, we were, once we were a leader and we are not quite leading the space anymore? We are, we're, we're, we are, we are, as a consequence of changes, we are now more out of the pack.

I would say we we're still ahead of almost everybody. Yeah. In terms of having that mandatory regime. Um, we are a smaller jurisdiction though with, uh, with more smaller firms. And, um, I think that the, the balance there has, has been, um, changed and is closer to what we see overseas.

But we are actually still ahead in terms of, um, the requirements that we have for reporting and the reporting that's being provided for New Zealand investors And, and the work that's been, the work that's been pushed in puts New Zealand in a really strong position to be able to, you know, even with the changes in the standards for our firms to be able to, uh, work in the international stage, uh, and to be able to, uh, adapt to any, if they're exporting to be able to adapt to any of the reporting requirements o overseas, I think it's,

it's been an interesting experience on the international stage. Uh, there's been, particularly with Scope three emissions and how you do reporting against those, there's been a real sense in which, at the international level, we've all been working together to try and learn from each other's experience and, and what is, what is going to work.

The potential risk of greenwashing is one that is as, as Liam said, we're going to have to keep a really strong eye on. But what has been clear from the international events that I've been part of is New Zealand's the work. Even though we are changing the standards, the work that we have done puts us ahead of, of, of other, of other jurisdictions.

A supplementary from Mar Williams. Yeah. Just a couple on the climate reporting and, um, Pathfinder, um, the climate reporting is meant to help investors price risk. Mm-hmm. Um, I'm interested in what feedback you've had from major shareholders particularly, um, you know, fund managers who are interested in pricing this appro pricing, uh, risk appropriately for their funds around this.

Um, and how you see your role as, um, supporting the market, um, for, for funds and for investment, incoming investment with this kind of information. If we won't, um, have some of that information being made available by legislation, do you anticipate more, um, sort of anomalies coming through, which you already identified?

I mean, the first round of, um, very helpful work that the FMA did identified that a number of reporting entities weren't reporting according to the standards already. Yeah. Um, well, the, I mean, major shareholders often themselves are fund managers. Um, and that goes through very quickly to the retail investors there.

The research we've done, the research that, um, we've seen from others tells us that ethical investment is still an important matter for a number of retail investors. Some of These guys are running green funds. Yeah. And that's, that they're, they're being, they're being offered in response to the demand. So it's important for them. And what they're promising that we've got, uh, very clear information around what is in, what is out, what are they investing in, um, are they sticking to their mandate.

And, um, we've consulted in the last few months on new guidance for ethical investment disclosure. We put out some initial guidance. Some years ago it was high level and, um, that was in the context of international standards still being in development.

We are seeing that is still the case, but, uh, a growing consensus in terms of international expectations. And so we've, we've put out, um, draft new guidance to provide clearer, um, assistance for those fund managers to tell the right story to the investors. Yeah. And one of the, um, one, one of the issues that the guidance is very sort of is, is very much picking up on, is, uh, you know, where entities wanting to do the right thing by way of offering green funds, but there is a risk that the marketing arm, uh, runs ahead

of what's actually being, what's actually being offered. And so one of the outcomes that we are looking for in the guidance, 'cause we have seen, uh, we, we have seen entities trip up over this is to say, it's great that you are, you know, that, that it's, it's, you know, it's, it's great that you want to go down this path, but it is important that you check that what you're saying in your marketing, uh, is actually matched, uh, pretty specifically to, I do, I just wanna dig in for this a little bit.

Okay. So, Uh, yeah, one supplementary, okay. Been in one supplementary. It's you sented Pathfinder for those misleading ethical claims, um, and, you know, identified gaps across the sector on these issues. Mm-hmm. Is greenwashing widespread in the New Zealand market?

We do not see that it's widespread. Um, but one of the things that is really challenging for retail investors is being able to compare different claims when there's no consistent terminology. Uh, when there are no baseline requirements. If I, if I say that I'm, uh, not investing in carbon then, but there's a fornot that says up to 5%, how, how clearly does that need to be said?

Or up to 15%? How clearly does that need to be said? How do we help investors to see that? How do we, um, ensure that companies, when they use terms, when they use terminology, they all mean the same thing. So until we get there, it's gonna be difficult to say who's right, who's wrong. Yeah. Um, this is a particular issue for Kiwis over funds.

Um, how do New Zealanders ha have some assurance that what they expect out of the ethical KiwiSaver funds, which many of us are invested in probably around this table because of our jobs, um, how can we assure ourselves that, um, this, this, there is some standard around what's being advertised and, um, what might all come out in the wash.

So I was just, so the guidance, it's a great, it's, it's a great question. And the guidance that we put put out is aimed to address just those to achieve Exactly. To achieve that point. Achieve that point. Yeah. Okay. Um, alright. I'll ask a supplementary here. So given the financial risk, uh, comes in many forms, it can be inflation, it could be geopolitics, it could be regulation.

So why, I mean, I'm thinking why is climate, um, signaled out for, uh, like a, a bespoke reporting regime? And in that regard, are you able to, uh, provide me an example, uh, where mandatory climate disclosures changed, uh, company's, um, real world behavior, um, in capital allocation or emission emission reduction?

Yeah. If you could give an example there. So I'll, I'll pick that up. It maybe that we need to come back to you with a specific, uh, sure. Specific example. So as the, the pulling up for a separate regime was a matter of government policy and that, that the standards were set by the XRB, and our job is to, uh, regulate and has been to regulate and enforce those.

So the reason, uh, but what we have, what we have seen, I would say broadly, not just in New Zealand, but elsewhere, is a focus on climate in particular, uh, probably due to a, a view that the, that, that the normal expectations that you would have from, uh, that you would have from entities properly looking at, you know, with any entities that are important in the economy, properly looking at their long-term risk that could arise from climate might be buried in the normal accounting standards.

And so there was a need for, uh, a new set of standards around climate. But this was, this, this was a, you know, this, this is a decision that was, uh, you know, that that was taken at the, at the government level, the focus, uh, on, you know, the, the focus on climate and the focus on ethical investing more generally is one, from a, from a regulatory perspective, when we're, when, when we are needing to work in an environment where securing a strong fund sector and a strong experience of investors, uh, in, in the fund sector, if there are particular elements

that become increasingly important to investors, so these can become aspects that can be marketed. So ethical investing, green investing, climate related, so it can all be part of something that is part of a marketing effort to attract investors to your fund and not to their fund. Yeah. That becomes something for us, uh, more generally outside of a climate, out of a climate, separate climate related disclosures regime, because that will be a risk to us that we will want to investigate to ensure that actually what is being held out to investors is

actually, is, is actually true. And that would be the case whether or not we had, uh, a climate related disclosures regime. I don't have an example, I'm afraid at the tip of my tongue. Do you Need That's fine. You can provide me, because what I want to look into it is that, is it, uh, a compliance just for compliance sake? Um, that's, that's the question. So yeah.

If you can provide an example later on, that's totally fine. Sure. We have a new primary from Uben Davidson. Thank you, uh, madam, she, um, artificial intelligence we know is becoming increasingly sophisticated. Um, so the policing and prevention around it, especially in this space, needs to, um, either keep up or preferably be ahead of it.

Mm-hmm. Um, how ready do you think we are domestically, and how do you think that compares, uh, internationally? So I might see if you want to pick up the Yep. The point, and then we can come onto Han to what we're doing. Sure, Sure. Um, we, I, I would say that, um, we're probably at risk of being slightly behind.

Um, we did some research work this year, uh, looking at the uptake and use of artificial intelligence in financial services firms. We found a large number of firms are interested in exploring, but, um, domestically less use than perhaps has been reported from some overseas markets.

It's also an area, though, where the development is incredibly rapid. Yeah. And the capability is developing very rapidly. It's becoming more and more useful for more and more things, um, uh, very quickly, and we will probably talk at some point, uh, about the work that we've been doing in the sandbox, but one of the things that we want to explore, um, there is opportunities for use of AI in the positive sense.

So, um, you know, opportunities potentially to improve access to financial advice. Yeah. Uh, but also what we need to do, uh, and we have, um, uh, work going on internally to make sure that we are skilled up enough to understand what are the guardrails that need to go along with that. Yeah. So we, um, so internally, uh, we are piloting the use of ai, uh, in, in internally.

Uh, I'm enjoying using it. Uh, it, but it can be quite clunky actually. The practical, practical use of how it can be clunky and requires human intervention is, uh, is, is is I think, important, an important part of the work that we will do. Uh, the opportunities, uh, of AI are definitely there.

I mean, looking at the work that one of our priorities this year is to look at the access to financial advice. You can see the long-term opportunities that there is with ai. What am I concerned about? I'm concerned that, uh, the historic lack of investment generally in technology, in the financial services sector in New Zealand, where we are still seeing basic problems with delivering what is promised by providers, creates a layer of risk over invest over technology that uses AI and the ability of New Zealand Financial Services Inc.

To be able to be across, uh, the, you know, where it can go, where it, where it can go wrong. And that will become an increasing focus for our work, both the opportunities, but also the approach that the sector needs to take to investing safely, uh, and, and testing the use of ai.

Yeah. So a A sub off that, I guess this could be the same, but it might be two different things. What advice would you give to government and what requests would you have of government around those challenges? Oh, gosh. I, we, we, we probably need to do some more research work on that, but, and initially, um, we, we've, we are fortunate, for instance, the financial advice, uh, laws were changed a few years ago, um, partly to allow, um, what was then called robo advice and what, what I suspect is now gonna be AI advice to be delivered.

So we've, we've got a relatively up to date and fairly flexible regulatory regime in that respect. Um, I, I may be proven wrong by, by the industry, but I don't think there are, um, specific regulatory barriers to ai. And I think with the introduction of Kofi, with our financial advice, um, supervisory regime, we've also got the tools to influence firms and how they use them.

Mm. But we are very, I mean, looking at the, uh, I mean, I know there's kind of issues around the pricing of AI stocks in the international markets at the moment, and is there a bubble or, or not. But we've got, um, we've got some great members of staff who are very future, future focused thinkers. And we are anticipating, uh, that over, whether it's the next year to five to 10 years, that ai, the use of AI in the financial services sector will dramatically change the way that financial services are received and offered.

And that means that we need, as an organization, we need to be keeping, uh, our eye kind of, you know, very firmly focused on the risks of today, but also the potential and risks of tomorrow, uh, that, that come through. So it will be, it will be, uh, increasing investment for us. Yep.

Supplementary from, yeah. Just a quick, um, question about the number of scam alerts you issued last year, I think was topped a hundred. Um, in, in terms of the use of AI within the organization, surely it's time that those scam alerts that aren't necessarily reaching the people who need to hear about them, vulnerable communities, older peoples, um, might be, you know, scanned for and delivered by ai.

Mm-hmm. Um, and so how do, how do you ensure that you're getting the investment that you need to be able to continue to deliver on? Yeah, so we are constantly, um, we, we've, we are constantly looking at ways in which we can improve a number of things when it comes to scam alerts, the way that we communicate them, the way that we work multi-agency with the scam alerts, the way that we use our own technology.

So we've been investing and we will continue to invest in our ability to be, uh, to, to, to be able to collect. It's, it's not just the actual scams, it's the ability to be able to liaise with agencies, both in New Zealand and internationally, to be able to collect intelligence that scams, scams are occurring.

But it, it's, it's con it, it, it will, there's not one fix that there will be to, it's a constant awareness that we need to keep being curious and inquisitive and resolved to be on top of the latest, um, not just the individual scams, but the ways in which, which the scams are appearing. Mm-hmm.

I Think it's encouraging that some of our regulated community are actively advertising to their constituent consumers around how to think about scams, basically slow the decision making down. Um, so that's, that's encouraging. And I think we can do more around educating consumers and investors about being aware of scams.

The person that's on the phone or the person on the screen is not necessarily the person you think it is. So it's actually raising people's awareness of potential financial loss earlier. Um, a new primary brief from Scott. Thank you. Uh, Madam Chair. Um, you talked about the risk of greenwashing earlier and, um, arena, I think mentioned the problem of verifying claims.

I'm wondering how well you work with others in the sector, such as charities like Mindful Money to help help us understand and help the, the great unwashed Yeah. Make decisions when there's such lack of clarity and, and, and, and people really concerned about where they invest, where the Kiwi Saver goes, what, what, what's happening with their money? Yeah.
